summary: Symbol-renaming-only PR that attempted to standardize fighter special-move callback names, especially airborne variants, from forms like SpecialNAir, SpecialSAir, SpecialHiAir, and SpecialLwAir to SpecialAirN, SpecialAirS, SpecialAirHi, and SpecialAirLw. The changes were broad and mechanical across fighter assembly labels, ftdata references, variables, and several C headers/source declarations. The PR was closed without merge and had no captured review discussion, so the repository outcome and rationale beyond the stated renaming intent are not evidenced.
what_changed: Renamed airborne special start-action symbols across many fighters so the Air qualifier appears before the special direction: for example ftCFalcon_SpecialNAir_StartAction became ftCFalcon_SpecialAirN_StartAction.
Applied the same naming rule for side, up, and down special airborne variants, such as SpecialSAir to SpecialAirS, SpecialHiAir to SpecialAirHi, and SpecialLwAir to SpecialAirLw.
Updated assembly global labels in character files including Captain Falcon, Donkey Kong, Fox, Ice Climbers, Kirby, Koopa/Bowser, Link, Luigi, Mewtwo, Peach, and others listed in the PR file set.
Updated centralized fighter data/reference assembly files, especially asm/melee/ft/ftdata.s and asm/melee/ft/code_800877F8.s, so callback references matched the renamed labels.

Updated matching C declarations and definitions in fighter headers and source files for already-decompiled or partially-decompiled fighters including Mario, Marth/Mars, Game & Watch, Pikachu, and Koopa.
The PR body explicitly stated: "this PR symbol renaming only"; the line counts were balanced at 280 additions and 280 deletions, consistent with pure renaming.
smart_moves: The rename was propagated through both definition sites and callback/reference tables, reducing the risk of broken symbol references from changing only assembly labels.
The naming pattern was applied mechanically across many fighters, which is appropriate for callback symbols that follow repeated per-character special-move conventions.
The proposed convention groups the airborne state consistently as SpecialAirX rather than treating Air as a suffix after the direction, making airborne variants easier to grep as a family.
Balanced additions/deletions and narrow one-symbol hunks in many files indicate the PR avoided mixing functional decompilation changes with naming cleanup.
decomp_lessons: When renaming global assembly symbols, update all consumers in data tables, external declarations, headers, and C source in the same change; fighter callback names often appear outside the defining character file.
For fighter special start actions, choose a convention that is grep-friendly and consistent across neutral, side, up, and down specials before large-scale migration.

Pure symbol-renaming PRs are easiest to validate when they preserve code bytes and only alter labels/declarations; balanced add/delete counts are useful supporting evidence but do not by themselves prove no behavioral change.
Large mechanical renames should be reviewed carefully against current project naming policy because this PR was closed unmerged and no review rationale was captured.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used direct assembly label/global renames without changing instruction bodies, as shown by hunks that replace only .global names and label names.
Preserved function addresses and code comments while changing symbol names, helping maintain match stability and traceability.
Updated ftdata callback references in tandem with per-fighter label names, which is necessary because fighter action tables can hold pointers to these start-action callbacks.
No evidence in the slice of new matching tricks, instruction reshaping, struct recovery, or control-flow changes; this was explicitly a symbol-only rename.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were captured in the PR context.
The PR was closed with merged_at null, but the provided slice does not state why it was not merged.
follow_up_queries: Check later merged PRs or current repository symbols to see whether the project eventually adopted SpecialAirN/S/Hi/Lw or kept SpecialNAir/SAir/HiAir/LwAir.
Search for remaining SpecialNAir, SpecialSAir, SpecialHiAir, and SpecialLwAir symbols before applying any similar mass rename.
Investigate why PR #408 was closed unmerged if deciding whether to reuse this naming convention.
